coverage_limits: The old selected option, feasible alternatives, and their latency and durability properties were not supplied.
Subject-revision stability is necessary but insufficient: the conclusion also depends on operator-change-2 remaining authoritative.
The record determines whether the old selection is presently settled, not which option a fresh durability-first synthesis would select.

rigor.trigger: A previously selected option is being justified across two viable priority rules.
rigor.tier_reason: Selection depends on an authoritative objective ordering, and that ordering changed even though the code and formal option properties did not.

decision_frame.question: May the latency-first selected option be treated as settled after authority changes the priority rule to durability-first?
decision_frame.system_boundary: Only the supplied scenario and artifacts/facts.json.
hard_constraints: Use only supplied scenario and artifacts.
Preserve the supplied fact that the subject revision and formal properties are unchanged.
Treat operator-change-2 as the current authority.
assumptions: A selected option is derived from both option properties and the authorized priority rule.
No dominance proof showing that the old option wins under every admissible priority rule was supplied.
decision_frame.uncertainty_posture: Reject the old settled status, but do not infer either a reversal or a new winner without the missing alternatives and property matrix.

coverage.reason: No relation, dependency, key, decomposition, or persistence schema is supplied.
No concurrent schedule, transaction history, isolation level, or anomaly is involved.
Latency and durability are objective labels here; no partition model, replication protocol, or consistency guarantee is supplied.
No algorithm, workload parameter, operation profile, recurrence, or index choice is supplied.
The old synthesis postcondition was established, if at all, under a latency-first precondition; the changed priority creates a different proof obligation.
No entropy, encoding, hashing, collision, or cardinality-estimation question is present.
A selected option is derived decision state; changing its authoritative priority input requires regenerating the synthesis rather than serving the stale result.
The pinned theory battery defines seven lenses and supplies no eighth family.
The pinned theory battery defines seven lenses and supplies no ninth family.

model.old_obligation: {priority = latency-first and properties = P} synthesize {selected = old-option}
model.current_obligation: {priority = durability-first and properties = P} synthesize {selected = current-option}

preconditions: The subject revision and formal property set P are unchanged.
steps: The old postcondition is scoped to the latency-first precondition.
Durability-first is not the same precondition.
A postcondition cannot be transported to the changed precondition without a refinement or invariance proof.
No such proof or fresh synthesis record is supplied.
derivations.result: The proposition that the old option is settled under the current authority is not established and is therefore refuted as stated.
derivations.residual_mismatch: This does not prove that the old option loses; it may remain selected after a valid new synthesis.

derivations.construct: Derived-data coherence and SSOT update anomaly

primary_theory: theory-battery.md §7: SSOT, derived versus base data, and materialized-view pattern

preconditions: The priority rule is a base input to synthesis.
The old selection was derived under latency-first authority.
steps: The selected option is derived from the decision-frame inputs.
operator-change-2 changes one authoritative base input.
Retaining the old derived selection without refresh creates the decision-record analogue of an update anomaly.
Coherence requires a new synthesis record tied to the current authority.
derivations.result: Serving the old selection as settled is stale-derived-state behavior.
derivations.residual_mismatch: The supplied material does not identify the old selection or provide enough option data to regenerate the winner.

model.selection_function: S(P,r) = the option preferred from property matrix P under priority rule r
model.known: P is unchanged and r changes from latency-first to durability-first

preconditions: At least two viable options may trade latency against durability.
No priority-invariant dominance result is supplied.
steps: P remaining fixed does not imply S(P, latency-first) equals S(P, durability-first).
Equality would require a new evaluation or a proof that the old option dominates on durability as well as latency.
Because the alternative set and property matrix are absent, inequality cannot be proved either.
derivations.result: The settled-status claim is refuted, while the fresh decision outcome remains underdetermined.
derivations.residual_mismatch: A reversal cannot be claimed from a priority change alone.

basis: The old synthesis, if any, discharged a different decision-frame obligation.
No current synthesis, dominance proof, alternative set, or property matrix is supplied.
conditions: A settled selection requires resynthesis under durability-first authority.
operator-change-2 must remain the authoritative priority source.
The alternatives and their durability and latency properties must be supplied.
concessions: The old option may still be selected if it is durability-optimal or priority-invariantly dominant.
Unchanged formal properties avoid recomputing those properties but do not avoid recomputing their priority-sensitive synthesis.
recovery_moves: Recover the option-property matrix and the prior decision record.
Issue a new synthesis record even if the selected option remains unchanged.
